findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that invokes `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` against a fixture transcript directory and asserts at least one labeled snapshot is written to the expected output path (e.g., via `ls` or `jq -e`).

AC1 tests the adapter fixture; AC2 tests the import guard; AC3 tests the enum. None exercise the script itself.
findings.finding: The backfill script `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` is listed as a new deliverable in Files but has no acceptance criterion verifying it can be invoked and produces correct output — the three ACs test only the adapter module.
findings.location: Files section: 'scripts/backfill_transcripts.py'; Acceptance Criteria section (all three ACs target the adapter unit tests)
scenarios: The adapter passes all tests but the backfill script has a broken argument parser or wrong output path and is never caught.
The script silently writes output to the wrong location; no AC detects this.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC for the calibration sub-sample (specifying sample size and acceptable false-positive rate threshold) or explicitly mark it as out-of-scope for this plan with a follow-up note.

No AC references the calibration sample, its size, the acceptable false-positive rate, or how it is checked.
findings.finding: The human-labeled calibration sub-sample that 'bounds the false-positive rate' is described in Approach as a real quality gate but has no corresponding acceptance criterion, proving command, or stated threshold — it is unverifiable as written.
findings.location: Approach section: 'a human-labeled calibration sub-sample bounds the false-positive rate'
scenarios: Executor treats this as aspirational prose and skips it; the plan completion is signed off without any false-positive bounding.
Executor spends significant effort building a calibration pipeline that was never intended to be delivered in this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` exists or will be created by this plan; if neither, update AC2 to target the actual module boundary where the core metrics command lives (or acknowledge the guard is forward-looking and describe how it will be enforced when that file is created).

findings.finding: AC2's portability guard asserts `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` does not import `adapters.claude_transcripts`, but that file does not exist in the codebase and is not listed as a file this plan creates. scenarios: The AC2 unit test runs `import src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` to check for a forbidden import — the file doesn't exist, so the test either errors or trivially passes, providing no real isolation guarantee.
A future `_commands/metrics.py` is created by a different ticket and accidentally imports the transcript adapter — the supposed portability guard never ran against the real file.
findings.suggested_fix: If the metrics command doesn't exist yet, reframe AC2 as a registry-only guard (already well-covered by the registry.py probe) and add a note that the portability test must be extended when `_commands/metrics.py` is created. Alternatively, if this plan is the one creating that file, add it to the Files section.

Codebase confirms `src/rebar/_commands/` has no `metrics.py`.
A `not-import` assertion against a non-existent module always passes, providing false safety.
The real isolation boundary is the existing `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py` — correctly named in AC2 — but the companion `metrics.py` guard is hollow.
findings.finding: The AC2 portability guard targets `src/rebar/_commands/metrics.py` but this plan does not create that file. The portability test gives false confidence: it passes in CI at commit time but the actual isolation is never validated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Give the calibration sub-sample an operational definition: specify (a) a minimum labeled-sample size or the artifact path where it lives, (b) the FPR threshold that must be met (e.g. ≤ 20%), and (c) whether the backfill script enforces this threshold or it is a one-time manual gate.
evidence: Approach: 'a human-labeled calibration sub-sample bounds the false-positive rate; every emitted value carries source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified and is segregated from structural series'
No calibration file path, format, threshold, or enforcement step is described anywhere in the plan.
AC1–AC3 do not include any calibration-related assertion.
The claim is presented as a safety mechanism but has no testable implementation path.
findings.finding: The 'human-labeled calibration sub-sample bounds the false-positive rate' statement has no operational definition — no threshold value, no artifact path or format for the calibration set, and no enforcement mechanism (e.g., script fails if FPR > X%). Without this, the calibration step cannot be implemented and the claimed safety property is unverifiable.
findings.location: Approach section: 'a human-labeled calibration sub-sample bounds the false-positive rate'
scenarios: Implementer skips calibration entirely (no artifact, no threshold) and the plan's FPR-bounding claim becomes purely aspirational.
Implementer builds calibration but against a wrong threshold (e.g. 50% FPR) because no target is specified, making the bound meaningless.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add per-value discriminating rules for each enum member (`env_setup`, `dependency`, `integration`, `tooling`, `none`) co-located with the enum definition in `claude_transcripts.py` (or a referenced prompt artifact), so the LLM classification is reproducible and the calibration sub-sample is meaningful.
evidence: Plan names the five enum values but provides zero discriminating rules between them
T8(a): schema referenced whose decision protocol is never co-located with it
T8(b): processing/decision rule missing — the LLM caller cannot reproduce consistent labeling without per-value definitions
No corresponding file or appendix in the plan specifies enum semantics
findings.finding: The classification enum (`env_setup`, `dependency`, `integration`, `tooling`, `none`) is named but no decision rules are defined to resolve ambiguous transcript excerpts — e.g., a pip install failure that also produces a missing-binary error could be `dependency`, `env_setup`, or `tooling`. The LLM prompt must invent these boundaries, making outputs non-reproducible and the false-positive bound meaningless.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The classification enum (`env_setup`, `dependency`, `integration`, `tooling`, `none`) and the JSON output schema are defined in the adapter'
scenarios: A transcript showing `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'xyz'` during a tool invocation is simultaneously `dependency`, `tooling`, and potentially `env_setup` — the LLM will pick arbitrarily
Two runs of the backfill on identical transcripts produce different enum assignments, undermining the 'calibrated backfill' claim
findings.suggested_fix: Add a docstring or prompt-template constant to `claude_transcripts.py` that defines each label: e.g., `env_setup` = Python version / virtualenv / PATH misconfiguration; `dependency` = missing or incompatible package; `integration` = service/API call failure; `tooling` = CI/build-tool failure; `none` = no env failure detected. Reference this table in the LLM prompt.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the grep error-signature set (e.g., `ModuleNotFoundError`, `ImportError`, `command not found`, `Permission denied`, `No module named`) in the adapter or plan, so the pre-filter is specified and the 'targeted pass' cost bound is enforceable.

No list of error signatures appears anywhere in the plan, Files, Tests, or ACs
T8(b): processing protocol (the grep filter) referenced but not co-located with any schema or specification

findings.finding: The 'grep error signatures' pre-filter — the central cost-bounding mechanism — is never enumerated anywhere in the plan. An executor cannot implement the grep pass without inventing the signature list, which directly undermines the claim that the adapter does a 'targeted pass, NOT a blind full-corpus LLM sweep'.
findings.location: Approach section — 'a targeted pass (error-signature grep first, then classify candidates)'
scenarios: An executor uses only `traceback` as the grep pattern, missing `ImportError`, `ModuleNotFoundError`, `Permission denied`, `command not found` — the targeted pass degrades to near-blind
Two implementers choose different signature sets and produce incomparable backfill corpora

This is a protocol referenced but not co-located with the schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete filesystem path(s) where Claude Code session transcripts are stored (e.g., `~/.claude/projects/<encoded-path>/<session-id>.jsonl`), cite the Claude Code version or docs URL that establishes this contract, add a discovery/enumeration step to the script design, and specify a fallback if the path is absent.

findings.finding: The plan never names the filesystem path where Claude Code session transcript files are stored, yet the entire adapter depends on being able to find and read them. The actual storage location (`~/.claude/projects/<project-id>/<session-id>.jsonl` or similar) is an internal Claude Code implementation detail that is not documented in the repo, has changed across versions, and is nowhere stated in the plan. The backfill script cannot be implemented without knowing this path, and no discovery mechanism, version guard, or fallback is described.
findings.location: ## Approach / scripts/backfill_transcripts.py
scenarios: Implementer invents or guesses the path incorrectly for the installed Claude Code version, silently finding zero transcripts
Claude Code changes its storage layout in a future release, silently breaking the backfill
A portability test passes (no import of adapter in core) while the adapter itself can never find data
findings.suggested_fix: Add an 'Inputs' subsection under Approach: 'Claude Code (≥ 2.x) stores session transcripts at `~/.claude/projects/<url-encoded-cwd>/<session-id>.jsonl`. The backfill script will glob `~/.claude/projects/$(python3 -c "import urllib.parse,os; print(urllib.parse.quote(os.getcwd(),safe=''))")/**.jsonl` and skip gracefully if none exist, logging the expanded path for debuggability.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the 'interval' vs 'snapshot' terminology collision: pick one term, define its JSON schema (e.g. fields: `{start_ts, end_ts, class, source, confidence}` for an interval OR `{ts, record: {...}}` for a point snapshot), and use that term consistently across Files, Approach, Tests, and AC1.
evidence: Tests: 'a transcript with a known env-failure signature is extracted and classified into an `env_setup` interval'
AC1: 'yields at least one classified interval tagged source=backfill_classified'
Files: 'scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — new: ... write labeled snapshots'
Existing `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py` defines snapshot as a timestamped point-in-time dict — no start/end duration fields.
A duration interval (start_ts, end_ts, class) and a point-in-time snapshot have different JSON schemas, affecting the output format, the jq assertion in AC1, and the enum test in AC3.
findings.finding: The plan uses 'interval' in the Tests and AC1 sections but 'snapshot' in the Files and Approach sections for the output entity the adapter emits. In the existing codebase, `snapshot.py` defines a point-in-time timestamped record; an 'interval' implies a time-range with start/end. These are structurally distinct schemas, so an implementer cannot determine which data structure to build.
findings.location: Tests section ('classified into an `env_setup` interval'), AC1 ('at least one classified interval'), vs Files section ('write labeled snapshots') and Approach section ('emitted value carries source=backfill_classified')
scenarios: Implementer builds a point-in-time snapshot (matching existing snapshot.py pattern) but the test asserts an 'interval' with start/end fields — test fails at first run.
Implementer builds an interval schema but the backfill script calls snapshot.write_snapshot() — type mismatch at runtime.

The JSON output schema is declared to be 'defined in the adapter' but its field list is never given, making AC3's assertion ('test asserts the enum has the documented values') unverifiable without reverse-engineering the implementation.

Evidence class: Unverified — integration exists in repo but the specific surface (which runner, which model, which auth env var) is unconfirmed for this new use
findings.finding: The LLM classification step ('classify candidates') names no interface, no model, no auth/HTTPS precondition, and no concrete cost-bounding mechanism. The repo has a full `rebar.llm` stack (runner, config, Anthropic backend), but the plan does not say whether the adapter uses it or a direct API call. Auth preconditions (e.g., ANTHROPIC_API_KEY must be set, HTTPS to api.anthropic.com) are entirely unstated.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'targeted extraction (grep error signatures) -> classify -> write labeled snapshots'
scenarios: Implementer uses a direct anthropic SDK call that bypasses the repo's retry/config/proxy layer, introducing a divergent auth path
No ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is set on the target machine and the backfill script crashes without a clear error
Classification runs on all ~N candidates with no cap, producing runaway API spend

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency strategy for the backfill script's output writes (e.g., overwrite-by-transcript-id, dedup on content hash, or explicit re-run guard) and add a test or AC asserting that running the script twice on the same fixture produces the same output count.
evidence: 'write labeled snapshots' — no deduplication or overwrite strategy mentioned
AC only checks that at least one interval is emitted for a fixture; does not test re-run idempotency
No mention of output file append vs. overwrite mode
findings.finding: The plan describes writing 'labeled snapshots' but does not address write idempotency — re-running the backfill script may produce duplicate entries in the output.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — ## Approach / ## Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Backfill is run twice (e.g., after adding more transcripts); duplicate classified intervals corrupt the historical series
Partial run followed by re-run doubles entries for transcripts processed in the first run

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the idempotency contract for `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`: specify whether output snapshots are overwritten (idempotent) or appended, and if appended, define a deduplication key so re-runs are safe.
evidence: Plan: 'scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — new: targeted extraction (grep error signatures) -> classify -> write labeled snapshots'
No idempotency, deduplication key, or overwrite/append policy is named anywhere in the plan.
T9 checklist: 'Shared/mutable state mutated atomically; operation idempotent under retry/at-least-once.'
findings.finding: The plan defines no idempotency contract for re-runs of `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`. If the script is run twice (e.g., after new transcripts accumulate), it is unspecified whether the output snapshots are overwritten, appended, or deduplicated. An append-on-rerun would silently double-count intervals; an overwrite could discard previously valid backfill data. Neither atomic write semantics nor a deduplication key are mentioned.
findings.location: Approach section / scripts/backfill_transcripts.py / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A developer re-runs the backfill script after adding more transcripts and silently doubles all previously classified intervals in the output snapshots, corrupting downstream metrics aggregation.
A CI job runs the script twice in the same workspace (at-least-once delivery) and produces non-deterministic snapshot counts.
findings.suggested_fix: Write output to a deterministically named file keyed by a content hash or run timestamp, or adopt an overwrite-by-default policy with an explicit `--append` flag; add a unit test asserting that running the extraction twice on the same fixture produces the same number of output intervals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in code or design doc) the enum evolution policy and prompt versioning strategy — at minimum, note that prompt changes require re-running calibration and that new enum values must be added to the portability-guard test's assertion.
evidence: 'The classification enum (env_setup, dependency, integration, tooling, none) and the JSON output schema are defined in the adapter' — hardcoded in module, no configurability mentioned
No mention of prompt versioning, config file, or extension mechanism for new enum values
findings.finding: The classification enum values (`env_setup`, `dependency`, `integration`, `tooling`, `none`) and the LLM prompt driving classification are rules expected to evolve, but the plan does not address how they are kept configurable or how enum additions are managed.
findings.location: ## Approach — classification enum definition
scenarios: A new category of env failure is discovered; adding it requires editing the adapter source and regenerating the calibration sub-sample with no documented process
The LLM prompt is tuned ad-hoc, breaking reproducibility of historical backfill runs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align terminology: pick one name ('interval' or 'snapshot') for the classified output entity and use it consistently across the Approach, Files description, Tests, and all ACs.
evidence: Tests: 'a transcript with a known env-failure signature is extracted and classified into an env_setup interval'
Approach: 'grep error signatures -> classify -> write labeled snapshots'
AC1: 'yields at least one classified interval tagged source=backfill_classified'
findings.finding: The output entity is called 'interval' in the Tests section and AC1, but 'snapshot' in the Approach section and Files description — the implementer cannot determine whether these are the same type/structure.
findings.location: Tests section ('classified into an env_setup interval'); Approach ('write labeled snapshots'); AC1 ('at least one classified interval')
scenarios: Implementer writes adapter emitting 'snapshots' but tests look for 'intervals', or vice versa, causing AC1 to pass/fail unexpectedly.
Schema defined in the adapter module (AC3) may not match what the script writes if the two terms refer to different shapes.
findings.suggested_fix: Use 'classified interval' (matching AC1/Tests) throughout; update Approach to read 'write labeled classified intervals' and update the Files description accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a maximum batch size and/or a checkpointed commit cadence for the LLM classification loop in `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` (e.g., classify and flush to disk in chunks of N candidates) so a failure mid-loop does not require reprocessing the entire candidate set.
evidence: ABSENCE: no batch-size parameter, throttle/sleep, or chunked-commit strategy is mentioned for the classify step.
Plan phrase: 'NOT a blind full-corpus LLM sweep' — cost is bounded by grep filter, but the resulting candidate set size is unconstrained and the classification is issued in one pass.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no batching or throttling for the LLM classification loop in the backfill script. Although the grep-first filter bounds the candidate set, the plan gives no upper bound on batch size or any inter-call delay, leaving open the possibility of a single large blocking run.
findings.location: Approach section — 'targeted pass (error-signature grep first, then classify candidates)'
scenarios: A repository with thousands of transcript files produces hundreds of grep-matched candidates; the classify step issues them all in one uninterruptible loop, and a failure mid-way leaves a partial write with no way to resume at the right offset.
findings.suggested_fix: Process candidates in configurable chunks (default 50); flush/commit each chunk's snapshots before starting the next, using the checkpoint manifest from the idempotency fix above.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling behavior for LLM call failures in the backfill script: at minimum, define whether the script retries with backoff, skips-and-logs the failing candidate, or halts — and ensure partial runs are detectable (e.g., a run manifest or idempotent output file).
evidence: 'targeted extraction (grep error signatures) -> classify -> write labeled snapshots' — pipeline described with no error-handling step
No mention of retry, backoff, or skip-on-failure for classify step
LLM calls are a hard external dependency with known transient failure modes
findings.finding: The backfill script makes LLM API calls (external integration) but the plan specifies no error handling, retry, or graceful-degradation behavior — it is unspecified whether a failed LLM call aborts the run, skips the candidate, or leaves partial output.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py — ## Approach
scenarios: LLM API returns a 429/500 mid-run; the script crashes after writing partial snapshots; re-running appends duplicates or overwrites valid data
Network timeout causes silent empty classification, emitting zero intervals for a transcript that should have had some — backfill silently under-counts
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap classify calls in a try/except that logs the failure and skips the candidate (continue), appending a `{source: backfill_classified, confidence: failed, reason: <error>}` entry to a separate error log so the run is auditable and re-runnable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/invalidation path for backfill snapshots: specify how stale snapshots are identified and replaced when re-classification occurs (e.g., a manifest with a calibration-version stamp, or a `--replace` flag that clears prior output before writing).
evidence: Plan: 'every emitted value carries source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified and is segregated from structural series'
Plan: 'a human-labeled calibration sub-sample bounds the false-positive rate' — implies calibration can change, but no re-classification or snapshot invalidation flow is described.
T9 checklist: 'There is a RETIRE/cleanup path — it doesn't leak/accumulate.'
findings.finding: The plan defines no RETIRE or invalidation path for accumulated backfill snapshot files. Over time, as calibration improves or transcripts are re-classified, stale snapshots with old confidence labels or superseded classifications will accumulate with no described cleanup, making it impossible to tell which snapshots reflect the current calibration baseline.
findings.location: Approach section / 'write labeled snapshots' / no retirement path mentioned
scenarios: Calibration is updated after discovering high false-positive rate; old snapshots remain on disk with the old (incorrect) classifications and are silently consumed by the aggregation layer alongside new correct ones.
Snapshot directory grows unboundedly across repeated backfill runs, with no mechanism to identify or prune superseded entries.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Drop the `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` sub-package and place `claude_transcripts.py` directly in `scripts/` (parallel to `backfill_bug_class.py`) rather than introducing a new sub-package namespace for a single, one-time-use adapter.
evidence: Zero existing call-sites for an `adapters/` sub-package under `src/rebar/metrics/`; all current metric modules (event_metrics.py, git_metrics.py, sidecar_metrics.py) import directly from each other without an adapters/ layer.
The plan's own justification is forward-looking: 'a parallel adapter could target another harness' — not a current done-definition.
`backfill_bug_class.py` in `scripts/` demonstrates the codebase's established pattern for one-off backfill logic: a flat script file, no sub-package seam needed.
findings.finding: The `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/` sub-package is a premature abstraction: it introduces a new directory/namespace for a single adapter with no existing call-sites, justified only by a hypothetical 'a parallel adapter could target another harness' — a future use that violates YAGNI and fails Rule-of-Three.
findings.location: Files section — `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py`, `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/claude_transcripts.py`; Approach section — 'a parallel adapter could target another harness'
scenarios: The sub-package directory is created for one file, then sits empty of other adapters indefinitely, becoming dead structure.
A future reviewer sees the adapters/ seam and assumes it's an extension point, adding unplanned adapters against unclear contracts.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `claude_transcripts.py` to `scripts/claude_transcripts_adapter.py` (or inline it into `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`). Remove `src/rebar/metrics/adapters/__init__.py`. If the plan genuinely anticipates a second adapter in this ticket's scope, name it explicitly in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the transcript adapter's classification enum with the existing `close_class` vocabulary in `common.schema.json`/`types.py` — either reuse `env_integration` (and others) verbatim, or explicitly justify and document why a distinct private enum is necessary for transcript-sourced intervals.
evidence: src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json already defines close_class with values: regression, plan_defect, env_integration, flaky, preexisting, not_a_bug, duplicate, escalated, undetermined.
src/rebar/types.py line 30: 'env_integration' is already a first-class vocabulary term.

scripts/backfill_bug_class.py classifies env_integration events from structural signals; the new adapter would classify similar env-failure events from transcripts under a different label ('env_setup') without explaining the divergence.
findings.finding: The plan's private classification enum (`env_setup`, `dependency`, `integration`, `tooling`, `none`) is a new vocabulary that partially overlaps the existing, already-closed `close_class` schema (`env_integration`, `plan_defect`, `regression`, …) in `src/rebar/schemas/common.schema.json` and `src/rebar/types.py`, and the existing `backfill_bug_class.py` already emits `env_integration`. Introducing a parallel private enum without justifying the divergence risks NIH-style vocabulary fragmentation.
findings.location: Approach section — 'The classification enum (`env_setup`, `dependency`, `integration`, `tooling`, `none`) … are defined in the adapter'; AC — 'test asserts the enum has the documented values'
scenarios: Downstream consumers (a future `rebar metrics` command or reporting layer) see both `env_integration` (from structural backfill) and `env_setup` (from transcript backfill) and must reconcile two overlapping categories.
The private enum quietly drifts from the closed schema vocabulary, making cross-source joins impossible.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard to `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` (e.g., a manifest/checkpoint file tracking processed transcript paths, or a dedup key on output snapshot IDs) so the script is safely re-runnable without double-applying.
evidence: ABSENCE: the plan contains no mention of a checkpoint file, a 'already processed' guard, deduplication on snapshot keys, or any idempotency mechanism.
Plan phrase: 'targeted pass (error-signature grep first, then classify candidates)' — describes a linear pipeline with no re-entrancy handling.
findings.finding: The backfill script has no idempotency or resumability guarantee. Re-running `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` after an interruption will re-process already-classified transcripts and can produce duplicate labeled snapshot entries in the output store.
findings.location: scripts/backfill_transcripts.py (Approach section / 'targeted extraction -> classify -> write labeled snapshots')
scenarios: Script is interrupted after writing N of M snapshots; re-run writes those N snapshots again, inflating counts and corrupting the calibrated series.
Operator runs the script twice to pick up newly discovered transcripts; all previously processed transcripts are double-counted.
findings.suggested_fix: Before writing each snapshot, check whether its canonical ID (e.g., SHA-256 of source transcript path + interval offsets) already exists in the output store; skip if present. Persist a checkpoint manifest so a killed run resumes from the last unprocessed transcript.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (in the plan and in `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py`) a back-out path: either a `--dry-run` flag that stages outputs before committing, or a `--rollback` / wipe-labeled-snapshots step, and assert the expected store state (zero backfill-classified entries) after rollback completes.
evidence: ABSENCE: the plan does not describe a rollback step, a dry-run mode, a staging directory, or a 'delete partially written outputs' procedure.
ABSENCE: T11 requires asserting schema/store STATE after rollback — the plan is silent on what the snapshot store should look like if the backfill is aborted or rolled back.
findings.finding: No back-out / rollback path is described. A partial failure mid-backfill leaves an unknown number of snapshot files written to the output store with no cleanup procedure, and the plan makes no assertion about the store's state after rollback.
findings.location: Approach section / Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: Backfill crashes after writing 40 of 100 snapshots; the store now contains a partial, non-reproducible subset that is indistinguishable from a complete run without manual inspection.
A bug in the classification enum causes mis-labeled snapshots; there is no described procedure to remove and re-generate them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the backfill-adapter pattern, the `source=backfill_classified`/`confidence=classified` labeling contract, and the segregation-from-structural-series invariant so future parallel adapters follow the same convention.
evidence: No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
'This LLM post-hoc classification is used only as a calibrated backfill (never a system of record)' — a novel data-provenance contract introduced without documentation
New pattern: source=backfill_classified/confidence=classified segregation from structural series
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern (optional LLM-classified backfill adapter with confidence labeling, segregated from structural series) but includes no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry capturing this decision.
findings.location: ## Approach / ## Files
scenarios: A future contributor adds another LLM-classified adapter without knowing the established contract, breaking the segregation invariant
The confidence-labeling convention is re-invented inconsistently in a parallel adapter
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0NNN-transcript-backfill-adapter.md` (or an AGENTS.md section) documenting: (1) why the adapter is quarantined, (2) the classification enum and its evolution policy, (3) the source/confidence labeling contract, and (4) the no-import-in-core invariant enforced by the portability-guard test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fourth AC to the leaf: `tests/unit/metrics/test_transcript_adapter.py` asserts `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` returns `False` and that backfill-classified values are excluded from the authoritative structural series — directly satisfying parent epic AC3.
evidence: Parent AC3: 'Every metric entry carries `source` and `confidence`, and a `source=backfill_classified` value is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series — a unit test asserts `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False and the segregation holds.'
Parent Success Criteria (5th bullet): '`is_authoritative()` returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series.'
Leaf ACs cover: (1) env-failure fixture classification, (2) portability guard, (3) enum values — none assert `is_authoritative()` behaviour.
The leaf is the sole producer of `backfill_classified` values in this adapter scope, making its test suite the natural home for this assertion.
findings.finding: The parent epic's Success Criteria and AC3 explicitly require that a unit test asserts `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` returns False and that segregation from authoritative structural series holds — covering values this leaf produces — but the leaf's three ACs omit this assertion entirely.
findings.location: Leaf AC list (all three ACs); Parent epic AC3: 'a unit test asserts `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False and the segregation holds'
scenarios: A sibling ticket may assume the leaf's tests cover `is_authoritative()` for `backfill_classified`, leaving no ticket that actually asserts it.
The parent's AC3 is a rollup-level gate; if no child ticket owns it, the epic cannot close cleanly against its own acceptance criteria.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the leaf's Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] A unit test in `test_transcript_adapter.py` asserts `is_authoritative("backfill_classified") is False` and that no `backfill_classified` value appears in an authoritative structural aggregation (satisfying parent epic AC3).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reference `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` as prior art; either extend/parameterize it for transcript inputs or explicitly justify why a second independent adapter is warranted (Rule-of-Three / structural incompatibility reason).
evidence: scripts/backfill_bug_class.py exists and implements classify_backfill() with source='backfill_classified', confidence='classified', quarantine-behind-script, non-authoritative by design
src/rebar/metrics/registry.py lines 21, 39, 44, 96 already register 'backfill_classified' as a valid, non-authoritative source
tests/unit/metrics/test_registry_heldout.py already tests backfill_classified behavior including is_authoritative==False
The plan does not mention backfill_bug_class.py or explain why a second parallel adapter is warranted rather than extending the existing one
findings.finding: The plan proposes a new `scripts/backfill_transcripts.py` + `adapters/claude_transcripts.py` implementing the backfill-classified pattern (same `source=backfill_classified`, `confidence=classified`, same quarantine-behind-script approach, same non-authoritative registry semantics) without referencing or building on the already-shipped `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` precedent and the existing registry/test wiring for `backfill_classified`. This is a reinvention of an established in-repo pattern, and the Rule-of-Three test is not addressed.
findings.location: ## Files / ## Approach / ## Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: An implementer creates a duplicate pattern with slightly different conventions, diverging from the already-established backfill contract and creating two competing adapters with implicit behavioral differences.
Future authors face two 'backfill_classified' sources with different structural conventions, breaking uniformity of the non-authoritative source contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a '## Prior Art' note citing `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` and `src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`'s existing `backfill_classified` wiring. If the transcript adapter follows the same output schema, share the common output-schema definition rather than redefining it in `claude_transcripts.py`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
